---
title: "American Burnweed (Erechtites hieraciifolius)"
slug: "erechtites-hieraciifolius"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/621862039/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "American Burnweed"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Asterales"
tax_family: "Asteraceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Asterales", "Asteraceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/621862039/medium.jpg" alt="American Burnweed" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/aline_morrow">
aline_morrow</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341581512">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Asterales
-   **Family:** Asteraceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=130888)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in disturbed areas such as recently cleared land, roadsides, and burned sites.

## Natural history notes

A fast-growing herb characterized by its upright stems, deeply lobed leaves, and clusters of small greenish to whitish flower heads. In Florida, it is commonly found in disturbed areas such as recently cleared land, roadsides, and burned sites. It grows during the warmer months and is an early colonizer of open habitats, often appearing quickly after disturbance.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/130888-Erechtites-hieraciifolius)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/907)
